retrieved_docs.title: Tooth Fracture -- Treatment / Management -- Crown-Root Fractures
retrieved_docs.content: Clinical and radiographic follow-up examinations should be conducted at 1 week, 6 to 8 weeks, 3 months, 6 months, and 1 year. After the 1st year, annual evaluations should continue for a minimum of 5 years.

retrieved_docs.title: Trauma to the Primary Dentition -- Treatment / Management -- Enamel-Dentine Fractures (uncomplicated crown fracture)
retrieved_docs.content: Managing an enamel dentin fracture with no pulpal exposure involves covering all exposed dentine with glass ionomer or composite. Follow-up clinical examination is recommended after 6 to 8 weeks; a radiographic examination is indicated only when clinical findings suggest pulp infection.

retrieved_docs.title: Trauma to the Primary Dentition -- Treatment / Management -- Crown-Root Fractures
retrieved_docs.content: The immediate management is to remove the loose fragment, often under local anesthesia, and assess if the crown can be restored. If the tooth is restorable and has no pulpal exposure, cover the exposed dentine with glass ionomer. If the tooth is restorable, but the pulp has been exposed, perform a pulpotomy or root canal treatment depending on the root development stage and fracture level.

retrieved_docs.title: Trauma to the Primary Dentition -- History and Physical -- Crown Fracture With Exposed Pulp (complicated crown fracture)
retrieved_docs.content: A complicated crown fracture involves enamel and dentine, plus the pulp is exposed. A periapical or an occlusal X-ray should be taken for diagnostic purposes and to establish a baseline.

retrieved_docs.title: Trauma to the Primary Dentition -- History and Physical -- Crown-Root Fractures
retrieved_docs.content: A crown-root fracture affects the enamel, dentine, and root; the pulp may or may not be exposed (complicated or uncomplicated). Additional findings may include loose but still attached fragments of the tooth. A periapical or an occlusal X-ray should be taken.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Emergencies -- Treatment / Management -- Tooth Fractures
retrieved_docs.content: Crown-root fractures are initially managed by identifying the extent of the fracture and exposing its margins. If still attached, the tooth fragment is removed by the dentist to evaluate for pulp involvement. If the pulp is not involved, the fragment can be reattached, or the exposed dentin can be covered with resin composite or glass ionomer.

retrieved_docs.title: Tooth Fracture -- Treatment / Management -- Crown-Root Fractures
retrieved_docs.content: If the pulp remains unexposed, the exposed dentin can be covered with glass ionomer or composite resin after retrieving the fragment. This conservative approach is viable as long as the fracture extends only slightly apical to the cementoenamel junction. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Emergencies -- Treatment / Management -- Tooth Fractures
retrieved_docs.content: If the tooth fragment is available, enamel fractures can be managed by reattaching the enamel fragment. If the tooth fragment is unavailable, the tooth edges can be smoothed if the lesion is small or restored with resin composite if the lesion is more extensive. It is recommended that patients follow up in 2 and 12 months so the dentist can assess the integrity of the restoration clinically and with radiographs. [49]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Emergencies -- Treatment / Management -- Tooth Fractures
retrieved_docs.content: The focus of initial treatment for enamel-dentin fractures is to protect the exposed dentin. Calcium hydroxide liners are commonly available in urgent care clinics, emergency departments, and primary care offices. These healthcare providers should apply this temporary treatment to prevent infection and protect the tooth’s viability. [58] In dental offices, calcium hydroxide can also be applied initially; however, the material must eventually be covered with a composite resin or glass ionomer. If the fragment is available, a dentist can consider reattaching it. If the fragment is unavailable, the dentist can perform restorative procedures using various materials, including resin direct composites or ceramics. [49]

retrieved_docs.title: Trauma to the Primary Dentition -- Treatment / Management -- Crown Fractures With Exposed Pulp (complicated crown fracture)
retrieved_docs.content: The treatment aims at preserving the pulp by partial pulpotomy. Local anesthesia is required, and a non-setting calcium hydroxide paste is applied over the pulp and covered with glass-ionomer and composite. For more extensive pulpal exposures, a cervical pulpotomy is indicated. Follow-up clinical examination is recommended after one week, six to eight weeks, and one year, with a radiographic examination at one year following pulpotomy.

retrieved_docs.title: Tooth Fracture -- Treatment / Management -- Enamel-Dentin Fracture with Pulp Exposure
retrieved_docs.content: The management of complicated crown fractures begins with addressing pulp exposure before reconstructing the lost dental structure. A conservative approach is preferred, as teeth can form a dentinal bridge after pulp exposure when treated with a pulp-capping material.

retrieved_docs.title: Tooth Fracture -- Treatment / Management -- Crown-Root Fractures
retrieved_docs.content: Treatment goals include exposing the fracture margins to facilitate bleeding control, maintaining moisture control during clinical procedures, and improving the patient's ability to manage plaque. Management begins with removing the tooth fragment to assess the full extent of the fracture and pulp involvement. If conditions allow, the fragment may be reattached later.

retrieved_docs.title: Tooth Fracture -- History and Physical -- Crown-Root Fractures
retrieved_docs.content: Recommended imaging includes a parallel periapical radiograph, 2 additional views with different vertical and horizontal angulations, and an occlusal x-ray. A cone-beam computed tomography (CBCT) is encouraged for a more detailed assessment of the fracture and treatment options. R oot Fractures

retrieved_docs.title: Tooth Fracture -- Treatment / Management -- Enamel-Dentin Fracture
retrieved_docs.content: If the tooth fragment is available, reattachment should be considered. The fragment must be soaked in water or saline solution for 20 minutes before the procedure to restore tissue hydration. Other treatment options include direct composite restoration, composite restoration with a waxup, and porcelain and resin-based indirect restorations.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Emergencies -- History and Physical -- Tooth Fractures
retrieved_docs.content: The classification of dental fractures is dependent upon the tissues involved. [9] Fractures from the most superficial to deepest include enamel infractions, enamel fractures, enamel-dentin fractures, enamel-dentin fractures with pulp exposure, crown-root fractures., and root fractures (See Image . Tooth Fracture).

retrieved_docs.title: Trauma to the Primary Dentition -- Treatment / Management -- Crown-Root Fractures
retrieved_docs.content: If the tooth is unrestorable, extract all loose fragments taking care not to damage the permanent successor, leave any firm root fragment in situ, or extract the entire tooth. When the tooth has been retained, a follow-up clinical examination is recommended after one week, six to eight weeks, and one year. A radiographic examination should be done one year following pulpotomy or root canal treatment.

This integrated framework supports comprehensive patient assessment, informed surgical planning, and coordinated management of fractures and bone‑density disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2107
retrieved_docs.content: Skull and face fractures, described in Arabic as كسر في قبو القحف, refer to injuries involving breaks in the cranial vault and the facial bones, encompassing a range of traumatic damage to the head and facial structures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 1184
retrieved_docs.content: effects as specific conditions associated with the concept of “كسر,” situates them within a broader category of unspecified injury sequelae, and further delineates limb‑specific fracture sequelae, thereby providing a structured framework for understanding and managing the long‑term consequences of various bone injuries.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2179
retrieved_docs.content: Osteoporosis/low bone mass (SMQ) includes several fracture‑related conditions and orthopedic interventions. Internal fixation of fractures (تثبيت داخلي للكسر) is a member of the osteoporosis/low bone mass category, reflecting that patients with reduced bone density often require surgical stabilization of broken bones. Hip head replacement (إعاضة رأس الفخذ) also belongs to this category, indicating that severe osteoporotic changes can lead to hip joint damage that necessitates prosthetic replacement. In addition, other fractures are listed as members of osteoporosis/low bone mass, underscoring the broad susceptibility of osteoporotic individuals to multiple fracture types. Recognizing these relationships helps clinicians anticipate and manage orthopedic needs in patients with osteoporosis or low bone mass.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 1 -- Community 122
retrieved_docs.content: (excluding hip or femur), and fracture or dislocation of the radius and ulna—and is an inverse‑ISA of “internal fixation of fracture” (تثبيت داخلي للكسر). A specialized form, **internal fixation of the clavicle without fracture reduction**, is also included. The “internal fixation of fracture” concept itself is a member of the osteoporosis/low‑bone‑density SMQ, reinforcing the association between osteoporotic bone and the need for fixation even when reduction is not performed. In the same SMQ, the concepts **إعاضة رأس الفخذ** (hip‑head prosthesis) and **كسور متعددة** (multiple fractures) are identified, underscoring that patients with reduced bone quality are prone to multiple fractures and may require prosthetic reconstruction of the femoral head. Collectively, these relationships illustrate an integrated framework in which surgical repair and fixation techniques for clavicular, humeral, radial, ulnar, scapular, and femoral injuries are organized under a shoulder‑arm reconstruction

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 1 -- Community 261
retrieved_docs.content: clavicle, femur, humerus, radius, scapula, and ulna—to the systemic disease category of osteoporosis or low bone density. “Fracture of neck of femur (hip)” itself is classified within the same osteoporosis SMQ, underscoring low bone mass as a key risk factor. Internal‑fixation procedures (identified as “تثبيت داخلي للكسر”) are associated with multiple treatment categories: “fracture or dislocation of hip and femur,” “fracture or dislocation of lower extremity (other than hip or femur),” and “fracture or dislocation of radius and ulna.” These associations emphasize that bone quality influences surgical decision‑making for both hip and peripheral limb injuries. Within the osteoporosis SMQ, additional members such as “جراحة مفصل الورك” (hip joint surgery), “إعاضة رأس الفخذ” (hip head prosthesis), and “كسور متعددة” (multiple fractures) illustrate the spectrum of interventions and clinical manifestations that arise in patients with compromised bone health. By connecting specific fracture

retrieved_docs.content: crash, is associated with extensive tissue damage, reinforcing the link between high‑energy fractures, tissue injury, and neurotoxic effects. Overall, fracture (كسر) integrates late sequelae, specific anatomical fracture types, associated tissue damage, co‑occurring injuries, procedural interventions, and psychosocial classifications, providing a comprehensive framework for patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 2 -- Community 877
retrieved_docs.content: The late effect of fracture of multiple bones, the late consequences of fracture of the skull and facial bones, and the late effects of fracture of the femoral neck are all described as being associated with a fracture (كسر). In addition, the open reduction of an open shoulder dislocation with fracture of the greater tuberosity is identified as a direct morphological manifestation of a fracture (كسر). Together, these statements highlight that both delayed sequelae across various skeletal regions and specific surgical interventions are directly linked to the underlying presence of a fracture, emphasizing the need for comprehensive assessment and management of fracture‑related pathology in patient care.

By connecting specific fracture entities, a comprehensive set of internal‑fixation and open‑reduction techniques, and hierarchical procedural categories to underlying bone‑density disorders, the knowledge base supports integrated treatment planning for orthopedic injuries of the hip, lower extremity, and shoulder‑arm region, while highlighting the importance of managing osteoporosis to reduce fracture risk and optimize surgical outcomes.

retrieved_docs.content: داخلي للكسر) is likewise a member of the osteoporosis/low‑bone‑density group and is applied across diverse skeletal sites, including the clavicle, hip, lower extremities (other than hip or femur), and the radius and ulna. Procedures and conditions related to osteoporosis extend beyond fracture fixation; hip‑head prosthesis (إعاضة رأس الفخذ) and multiple fractures (كسور متعددة) are also members of the osteoporosis SMQ, illustrating how severe bone‑density loss can lead to joint replacement and recurrent fractures. Collectively, these relationships demonstrate that clavicular and shoulder‑arm surgical repairs are precisely linked to shoulder anatomy, that femoral intracapsular fractures correspond to hip neck fractures and are described in multiple languages, and that the classification of closed fractures and various fixation techniques is tightly connected to osteoporosis‑related bone fragility. This integrated framework supports comprehensive patient assessment, informed surgical

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 2 -- Community 1085
retrieved_docs.content: Internal fixation of bone without fracture reduction is a specific form of internal fixation of fracture (تثبيت داخلي للكسر) and is employed as a treatment for fractures or dislocations of the hip and femur, for other lower‑extremity fractures (excluding the hip or femur), and for fractures of the radius and ulna. This procedure, together with other fracture‑related interventions such as hip‑head prosthetic replacement (إعاضة رأس الفخذ) and the broader category of “other fractures,” is classified within the osteoporosis/low bone mass syndrome (هشاشة العظام/قلة العظام SMQ). Consequently, these surgical treatments are particularly relevant for patients with reduced bone density, highlighting the close relationship between osteoporotic bone disease and the need for internal fixation or prosthetic reconstruction of various skeletal sites.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 1 -- Community 230
retrieved_docs.content: hip biomechanics. Additionally, “Internal fixation of bone without fracture reduction” is identified as a versatile internal fixation technique applicable to fractures or dislocations of the hip and femur, the radius and ulna, and other lower‑extremity sites, underscoring its role across multiple skeletal regions. Collectively, these entities illustrate an integrated framework for fracture fixation, deformity correction, growth modulation, prosthetic revision, and preventive care in thigh and leg surgery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 2 -- Community 1032
retrieved_docs.content: Closed fracture of intracapsular section of femur is mapped to fracture of neck of femur (hip), indicating that these two descriptions refer to the same clinical injury of the proximal femur. Fracture of neck of femur (hip) is listed as having a member relationship with هشاشة العظام/قلة العظام (SMQ), the standardized query that groups adverse events related to osteoporosis or low bone density. In the same SMQ, several procedural and injury concepts are also classified as members: تثبيت داخلي للكسر (internal fixation of a fracture), إعاضة رأس الفخذ (femoral head prosthesis or hip replacement), and كسور متعددة (multiple fractures). Together, these relationships show that hip neck fractures, their surgical management (internal fixation or prosthetic replacement), and the occurrence of multiple fractures are all recognized as clinical manifestations or treatments associated with osteoporosis/low bone density, underscoring the importance of bone health assessment and appropriate orthopedic intervention in patients with compromised skeletal integrity.
